attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with new-onset diplopia and ataxia that began three hours earlier. This was accompanied by a shock-like sensation when she flexes her head. Her medical history is unremarkable and she takes no regular medications.

On examination, there is a right abducens nerve palsy. Her vision in the right eye is reduced, with poor recognition of the colour red. When light is shone in her right eye, there is no constriction in either eye. When light is shone in her left eye, both pupils constrict. There is no nystagmus. The remainder of her neurological examination is unremarkable.

A gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the brain and spinal cord is performed which shows multiple small non-enhancing lesions along the brainstem, and three enhancing lesions along the cervical region. She is hospitalised and receives intravenous methylprednisolone for three days. Upon symptom resolution, she is discharged from hospital.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate to offer her?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation of diplopia and ataxia is highly suggestive of a central nervous system disorder that involves the brainstem. Furthermore, it is likely she has optic neuritis manifesting with blurry vision, reduced colour detection, and a relative afferent pupillary defect. She also describes Lhermitte phenomenon, a shock-like sensation when she flexes her head. These are all symptoms that are highly suggestive of multiple sclerosis (MS). Such a diagnosis is confirmed via the gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the brain and spine, which showed both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions (indicating both currently active and older lesions) in different regions of the central nervous system, fulfilling the dissemination in space and time criteria for MS.

Treatment of severe acute MS consists of intravenous methylprednisolone (1 gram for 3 days). Patients with steroid-refractory relapse require plasma exchange. After managing the relapse, all patients should be considered for disease-modifying agents if a diagnosis of multiple sclerosis is suspected. These agents include interferons, glatiramer acetate, monoclonal antibodies, and teriflunamide. Although the interferons and glatiramer acetate are older drugs, they remain the first-line treatment and hence would be the correct option in this case. However, patients at a higher risk of disability might be started on more potent therapies, such as alemtuzumab, natalizumab and ocrelizumab.

Interferon-ß-1a is an immunoregulatory molecule that is responsible for antagonism of gamma interferon, reduction of cytokine release and augmentation of suppressor T cell function. It is a first-line agent in multiple sclerosis.

Watchful waiting can be used in clinically isolated syndrome, where there is insufficient evidence to fulfill the diagnosis of MS. This involves monitoring future central nervous system symptoms. However, since the patient fulfils the diagnosis of MS, she will require disease-modifying therapies.

Prednisone is a corticosteroid with anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects by regulating gene expression of the immune cells. Due to its side effect profile with long-term use, it is not used in disease-modifying treatment of MS, but only in the context of acute relapse.

Vitamin B12 tablets are used when patients have vitamin B12 deficiency. Vitamin B12 is an essential vitamin for the production of pyrimidines and the conversion of methylmalonic acid to succinyl-CoA. In patients with vitamin B12 deficiency, there is accumulation of methymalonic acid, which has been postulated to damage myelin sheath resulting in neurological symptoms akin to those seen in this patient. However, vitamin B12 deficiency is unlikely to be the cause of her neurological symptoms.

Amantadine is used to manage fatigue in MS but is not a disease-modifying therapy. It does not alter the course of the disease or prevent relapses, which is why it is not the appropriate choice for long-term management in this patient.
learningPoints: In patients with MS, interferon-ß-1a and glatiramer acetate remain the first line of treatment.
Amantadine is used to manage fatigue in MS but is not a disease-modifying therapy.
